source: Model answers for 6 wines: Rustenberg Chardonnay, Ata Rangi Pinot Noir, Ca di Alte Pinot Grigio, Château Dutruch Bordeaux, Château Doisy-Daëne Barsac, 16 Stops Shiraz

nose.structure: 1. Intensity statement. 2. Primary aromas (list). 3. Secondary aromas (list with source implicit or explicit). 4. Tertiary aromas (list with source implicit or explicit). 5. Development stage.
nose.example: Medium(+) intensity. [Primary] lemon, peach, melon, pineapple. [Secondary] biscuit, bread, vanilla, cloves. [Tertiary] honey, nutty. Developing.
nose.ordering_principle: Primary first, then secondary, then tertiary; development last

palate.structure: 1. Sweetness–acidity–[tannin]–alcohol–body scale values. 2. Intensity value. 3. Flavour descriptors (primary/secondary/tertiary). 4. Finish length.
palate.example: Dry. Medium(+) acid. Medium alcohol. Full body. Medium(+) intensity. [Primary] lemon, peach, melon, pineapple. [Secondary] biscuit, bread, vanilla, cloves. [Tertiary] honey, nutty. Medium(+) finish.

conclusions.structure: Quality level + readiness statement (1 mark each)
conclusions.example: Very good quality. Drink now but has potential for ageing.

nose.structure: Intensity. Primary (specific). Secondary (identified source: oak/MLF). Tertiary (bottle age). Development.
nose.example: Pronounced intensity. Red cherry, redcurrant, raspberry, red plum, violet, black cherry. Vanilla, cedar, smoke, cloves. Earth, forest floor, dried cranberry, mushroom. Developing.

palate.structure: Dry – high acid – medium tannin – high alcohol – medium(+) body – pronounced intensity – [primary/secondary/tertiary flavours] – long finish.

outstanding_red.conclusions: Outstanding quality. Drink now but has potential for ageing.

simple_wine.wine_example: Ca di Alte Pinot Grigio 2024 / 16 Stops Shiraz
simple_wine.key_difference: Explicitly state the wine is SIMPLE in nose and palate sections

nose.strategy: Intensity. State: simple. List primary aromas. Development.
nose.example: Medium(-) intensity. Simple. Pear, green apple, white peach. Young.
nose.note: 1 mark for 'simple' + 4 marks for valid primary aromas; no secondary/tertiary expected

palate.strategy: Scale values. State: simple. List primary flavours. Finish.
palate.note: 1 mark for 'simple' + 2 marks for primary flavours

simple_wine.conclusions: Quality: aceptable. Readiness: drink now, no ageing.

aged_sweet_white.nose: Pronounced intensity. Developing. [Primary] honeysuckle, peach, apricot, pineapple, mango. Baked/stewed fruits, raisin. [Secondary] vanilla, cloves, cedar. [Tertiary] honey, almond, marmalade, dried apricot.
aged_sweet_white.palate: Sweet. High acidity. High alcohol. Full body. Pronounced intensity. [same descriptors as nose]. Long finish.
aged_sweet_white.conclusions: Outstanding. Drink now but has potential for ageing.
aged_sweet_white.key_insight: Sweet wines require 'sweet' as sweetness scale value; high acidity despite sweetness is key structural signal

information_ordering_principles: SAT order is mandatory: Appearance → Nose → Palate → Conclusions
Within nose: intensity first, aroma categories in primary/secondary/tertiary order, development last
Within palate: structural elements (sweetness, acidity, tannin, alcohol, body) before flavour descriptors, finish last
Development stage and quality level must be consistent with each other
